chunk_summary: This section provides standardized counselling scripts to address feeding difficulties, correct myths, and implement structured feeding strategies for children aged 1–5 years, emphasizing measurable plans and follow-up gates.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of consistent counselling scripts in managing feeding difficulties.
Learn strategies to stop force feeding, screen feeding, and excessive milk consumption.
Identify and correct common feeding myths to improve child nutrition and growth.
Implement structured feeding plans with follow-up gates for effective monitoring.
key_facts: Force feeding worsens refusal and slows learning; caregivers decide food offered, children decide quantity.
Milk intake for children aged 1–5 years should be capped at 500–600 mL/day to prevent anemia and solid food displacement.
Structured snacks with 2–3 hour gaps between eating times prevent grazing and improve meal hunger.
Tonics do not fix appetite issues caused by poor meal structure, texture progression failure, or milk displacement.
Complementary foods are required after 6 months; by 12–23 months, children should eat family foods with milk as an adjunct.
reference_quote: Milk is nutritious, but too much milk replaces solid food and can worsen anemia. For ages 1–5 years we cap milk at about 500–600 mL/day and stop bottle feeding. (Source: NICE NG75)
document_key_topics: Structured feeding plans and counselling scripts
